The motivation for our project comes from the growing need for a specialized writing as-
sistantthathelpsinenhancingthelinguisticqualityofthementionedtechnicaldocuments
while preserving the everything the author wants to uniquely present. In academic and research
contexts, precise language, grammatical accuracy, and clarity are extremely essential for effec-
tively communicating complex ideas.Errors in language or inconsistencies in style can lead to
misunderstandings, reduce the impact of the research findings, and even hinder acceptance by
journals or conferences.
Existing AI-driven writing tools often emphasize fluency, readability, or stylistic enhance-
ment, that, even while being helpful in general writing, can introduce significant challenges in
the writing of such important technical documents.
The currently available AI tools are not always able to identify grammatical error in large
academicmanuscriptsliketechnicaldocumentsorresearchpapers.Theyarenotabletopin-
point the errors and therefore are not able to correct these errors.
Due to these errors not being corrected,the documents are flagged and therefore can lead
to loss of credibility and, further, even rejection by high-level journals and institutions.
Given these challenges,there is a clear need for a tool that emphasizes correction of even
the smallest errors.Such a tool should focus on identifying and rectifying grammatical errors,
punctuation issues, and other linguistic inconsistencies without altering the meaning or style of
the original text.By doing so, it would enable researchers to produce manuscripts that are both
linguistically accurate and authentically human-authored.
The broader motivation behind this project is to empower researchers to communicate their
findingsconfidently,ensuringthattheirmanuscriptsmeethighstandardsofclarity,accuracy,
and integrity.By providing a system that enhances correctness without compromising original-
ity,this project aims to support the production of high-quality scientific work that is resistant
toAI-detectionconcerns,preservesauthorialstyle,andupholdstheprinciplesofacademic
integrity.Ultimately,thisapproachcontributestotheadvancementofscholarlycommunica-
tion,enablingresearcherstofocusonthecontentoftheirworkratherthanthemechanicsof
language.

Theliteraturereviewrevealsseveralcriticalfindingsregardingcurrentgrammarcorrection
tools and their applicability to academic writing:
Generative AI Risks
The analysis demonstrates that generative AI-driven writing tools,despite their market domi-
nance, present substantial risks for researchers.These tools may trigger academic misconduct
concerns, compromise the original authorial voice, and activate AI detection systems employed

by publishers and academic journals .

correctionovercontentrewriting.Theminimaleditapproach,whichfocusesonpreserving
theauthor’soriginalintentwhilemakingonlynecessarycorrections,alignswithestablished
grammatical error correction (GEC) research recommendations .
Academic Integrity vs.Feature Creep
As AI tools incorporate increasingly sophisticated generative capabilities, their risk profile for
scholarlycommunicationescalatesproportionally.Onlyacorrection-firstmethodologycan
guarantee document authenticity and maintain authorial ownership of the work .
Ethical and Technical Obstacles
Thereviewidentifiesseveralsystemicchallengesincludingethicalexposures,databias,hal-
lucinations,andlimitationsincontextandtonecontrol.Theseissuesnecessitatecontinued
human oversight and specialized tool development for research writing contexts .

Thecomprehensiveanalysisrevealsagenuinemarketgapforhigh-precision,non-generative
grammarcorrectiontoolsspecificallydesignedforscientificwritingcontexts.Currentmar-
ket leaders prioritize generative features that may compromise academic integrity,potentially
exposing researchers to AI detection risks and unintended style alterations.
The proposed project’s distinctive value proposition lies in its strict adherence to the mini-
mal edit mandate—implementing only necessary corrections without paraphrasing or rewriting
content.This approach maintains fidelity to the original manuscript while enabling researchers
toproduceerror-free,authenticallyhuman-authoreddocumentsthatmeetthetruststandards
required by publishers and academic institutions.

The aim of this project is to develop a writing assistant that enhances the grammatical ac-
curacy and lexical precision of scientific manuscripts while preserving the researcher’s original
style and ensuring compliance with academic integrity standards.To achieve this,the project
sets out the following specific objectives:
1.Developahigh-precisionGrammaticalErrorCorrection(GEC)engine:Thecore
objectiveistobuildaGECsystemspecificallytailoredforscientificwriting,witha
focus on widely accepted formatting and style guidelines.Unlike general-purpose gram-
mar checkers, this system will account for the formal structure, syntax, and conventions
commonlyusedinacademicmanuscripts.Theenginewillaimtodetectandcorrect
grammatical, punctuation, and structural errors with a high degree of accuracy, minimiz-
ing false positives and ensuring that suggested corrections do not introduce ambiguity or
alter the intended meaning of the text.
2.Implementaminimal-editstrategy:Thesystemwilladoptaconservativecorrection
approach,targetingonlyclearandunambiguouserrors.This“minimal-edit”principle
ensuresthatchangesaremadesolelywherenecessary,preventingover-correctionor
stylistic alterations that could compromise the author’s original expression.
3.Designasingle-wordsuggestionmodule:Inadditiontogrammaticalcorrection,the
project aims to implement an enhancement feature that provides contextually appropriate
wordsuggestions.Thismodulewillfocusonsuggestingstrongerverbs,concisealter-
natives,ormoreprecisescientificterminology.Thegoalistoenrichthemanuscript’s
vocabulary and clarity while preserving the original sentence flow and authorial style.
4.Evaluatesystemperformanceusingprecision-focusedmetrics:Thesystemwillbe
assessedusingmetricsthatprioritizeprecision,whichweighsprecisionmoreheavily
thanrecall.Thisevaluationstrategyalignswiththegoalofbuildingusertrust,asre-
searchers are more likely to accept a tool that makes accurate, reliable corrections rather
than one that over-corrects or generates questionable suggestions.
5.Ensurecompliancewithacademicintegritypolicies:Akeyobjectiveistoavoid
sentence-levelrewriting,oranyformofAI-assistedcontentthatcouldbeflaggedas
11
non-original.Bystrictlylimitinginterventionstogrammaticalandlexicalcorrections,
the system safeguards the author’s originality and helps maintain compliance with insti-
tutional and journal policies regarding AI usage in academic writing.
6.Promote consistency and clarity in scientific communication:Beyond technical cor-
rections, the system aims to contribute to overall manuscript quality by ensuring consis-
tent usage of terminology, punctuation, and style throughout the document.By improv-
ing both micro-level grammatical accuracy and macro-level coherence, the tool supports
clearer, more professional communication of research findings.
Through these objectives, the project aims to create a comprehensive, reliable, and integrity-
conscious writing assistant that addresses the limitations of existing AI-driven tools while en-
hancing the productivity, confidence, and output quality of researchers and academics.

Technical information has been produced and disseminated at an unprecedented rate in re-
cent years. Modern innovation and knowledge sharing are based on academic research articles,
university dissertations, project reports from the industry, software documentation, and techni-
cal manuals. This has led to a significant increase in the demand for technical writing that is
grammatically correct, clear, and succinct. The pioneer of readability studies, Robert Gunning,
emphasized that complex subjects need exceptional clarity for correct comprehension rather
than complex writing (Gunning, 1968). Technical documents are crucial for conveying com-
plex information, and their quality is crucial. However, they often have grammatical errors,
stylistic inconsistencies, or ambiguous wording. Such issues can severely undermine the credi-
bility of the document, create critical misunderstandings, and in high-stakes environments like
engineering or medicine, lead to expensive or even dangerous problems (Allen, 2012).
From simple spell checkers to complex grammar correction systems, automated writing
assistance has evolved over time. In the past, traditional grammar checkers—which are fre-
quently built into word processors—used rule-based techniques, identifying errors using a set
of manually created grammatical rules (Knight & Chander, 1994). Although these systems are
good at fixing basic errors, they are fragile and frequently fall short in understanding context,
particularly in specialised fields. By treating grammar correction as a ”translation” task from
a ”incorrect” English sentence to a ”correct” one, statistical methods—in particular, Statistical
Machine Translation (SMT)—marked a significant improvement and enabled systems to learn
patterns from vast text corpora (Brockett et al., 2006). However, these models still struggled
with long-range dependencies and subtle semantic context. This creates a distinct and press-
ing need for a specialized grammar correction system meticulously designed for the rigors of
technical and academic documents.
This requirement results from generic tools’ inability to manage the particular difficulties of
technical writing. Specialised terminology and jargon are a significant problem; generic tools
that are trained on general text frequently mistakenly identify legitimate technical terms as er-
rors. The prevalence of complex sentence structures presents another major obstacle. Long
sentences with several subordinate clauses are common in technical content, and these sen-
tences are prone to subtle grammatical errors that traditional checkers usually miss (Leacock
5
et al., 2010). Additionally, generic tools are not intended to enforce the stylistic conventions
of technical writing, which include a preference for objective language and a formal tone (Day
& Gastel, 2016). Even slight ambiguity can cause misinterpretation in technical contexts, with
serious repercussions. In academia, poor grammar can diminish the perceived quality of a
manuscript, potentially leading to rejection from high-impact journals (Wallwork, 2016). For
international researchers and students who may not be native English speakers, these challenges
are amplified, creating an additional barrier to disseminating their work.
To address these constraints, our initiative seeks to create a sophisticated grammar cor-
rection tool that focuses on technical documents by utilizing cutting-edge Natural Language
Processing (NLP) methods. The suggested system will utilize transformer-based neural archi-
tectures, whose self-attention mechanism transformed NLP by allowing models to grasp intri-
cate, long-range dependencies and context (Vaswani et al., 2017). Our approach will be based
on a pre-trained language model, like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) or T5 (Raffel et al., 2020).
The essence of the project is to optimize this model using a targeted collection of technical
and scholarly documents sourced from platforms such as arXiv.org and PubMed Central. This
domain adaptation process will adjust the model to the unique vocabulary, syntax, and style
of scientific literature, enabling it to accurately manage technical terms that generic models
struggle with (Beltagy, Lo, & Cohan, 2019).
The initiative will encompass multiple essential phases. Initially, we will compile a pre-
mium dataset of technical writing, forming parallel pairs of ”correct” and ”incorrect” sentences
through both synthetic error creation and current annotated corpora such as the BEA shared
task datasets (Bryant, Felice, & Briscoe, 2017). Next, we will refine the selected transformer
model on this dataset, training it to not only correct grammatical mistakes but also to pro-
pose stylistic enhancements that improve clarity and brevity. Ultimately, the evaluation of the
model’s performance will be conducted meticulously using established metrics for Grammat-
ical Error Correction (GEC), including the M² Scorer (Dahlmeier & Ng, 2012), along with
usability assessments involving target users.
The primary goal of this project is to close the significant divide between standard gram-
mar checkers and the specific requirements of the technical and academic sectors. Our tool
6
aims to considerably lessen the time and effort people invest in proofreading technical docu-
ments by offering precise, context-sensitive, and style-aware corrections. It will equalize access
to premium editing, offering a significant resource for individuals who might lack access to
skilled human editors. By doing so, this initiative could serve as a dependable tool for creating
accurate, high-quality technical documents, thus enhancing clearer scientific communication,
essential for the progress of knowledge and technology.
7
The motivation for our project comes from the growing need for a specialized writing as-
sistant that helps in enhancing the linguistic quality of the mentioned technical documents
while preserving the everything the author wants to uniquely present. In academic and research
contexts, precise language, grammatical accuracy, and clarity are extremely essential for effec-
tively communicating complex ideas. Errors in language or inconsistencies in style can lead to
misunderstandings, reduce the impact of the research findings, and even hinder acceptance by
journals or conferences.
Existing AI-driven writing tools often emphasize fluency, readability, or stylistic enhance-
ment, that, even while being helpful in general writing, can introduce significant challenges in
the writing of such important technical documents.
The currently available AI tools are not always able to identify grammatical error in large
academic manuscripts like technical documents or research papers. They are not able to pin-
point the errors and therefore are not able to correct these errors.
Due to these errors not being corrected, the documents are flagged and therefore can lead
to loss of credibility and, further, even rejection by high-level journals and institutions.
Given these challenges, there is a clear need for a tool that emphasizes correction of even
the smallest errors. Such a tool should focus on identifying and rectifying grammatical errors,
punctuation issues, and other linguistic inconsistencies without altering the meaning or style of
the original text. By doing so, it would enable researchers to produce manuscripts that are both
linguistically accurate and authentically human-authored.
The broader motivation behind this project is to empower researchers to communicate their
findings confidently, ensuring that their manuscripts meet high standards of clarity, accuracy,
and integrity. By providing a system that enhances correctness without compromising original-
ity, this project aims to support the production of high-quality scientific work that is resistant
to AI-detection concerns, preserves authorial style, and upholds the principles of academic
integrity. Ultimately, this approach contributes to the advancement of scholarly communica-
tion, enabling researchers to focus on the content of their work rather than the mechanics of
language.
8
This expanded literature review provides a comprehensive scholarly comparison for techni-
cal document grammar correction, integrating detailed analysis of market trends and strategic
implications. The review examines current tools and approaches, highlighting their strengths,
limitations, and relevance to academic integrity in scientific writing.

Key Discussions
The literature review reveals several critical findings regarding current grammar correction
tools and their applicability to academic writing:
Generative AI Risks
The analysis demonstrates that generative AI-driven writing tools, despite their market domi-
nance, present substantial risks for researchers. These tools may trigger academic misconduct
concerns, compromise the original authorial voice, and activate AI detection systems employed
9
by publishers and academic journals .
Minimal Edit Philosophy
A significant gap exists in the industry for specialized assistants that prioritize grammatical
correction over content rewriting. The minimal edit approach, which focuses on preserving
the author’s original intent while making only necessary corrections, aligns with established
grammatical error correction (GEC) research recommendations .
Academic Integrity vs. Feature Creep
As AI tools incorporate increasingly sophisticated generative capabilities, their risk profile for
scholarly communication escalates proportionally. Only a correction-first methodology can
guarantee document authenticity and maintain authorial ownership of the work .
Ethical and Technical Obstacles
The review identifies several systemic challenges including ethical exposures, data bias, hal-
lucinations, and limitations in context and tone control. These issues necessitate continued
human oversight and specialized tool development for research writing contexts .
0.0.2
Conclusion
The comprehensive analysis reveals a genuine market gap for high-precision, non-generative
grammar correction tools specifically designed for scientific writing contexts. Current mar-
ket leaders prioritize generative features that may compromise academic integrity, potentially
exposing researchers to AI detection risks and unintended style alterations.
The proposed project’s distinctive value proposition lies in its strict adherence to the mini-
mal edit mandate—implementing only necessary corrections without paraphrasing or rewriting
content. This approach maintains fidelity to the original manuscript while enabling researchers
to produce error-free, authentically human-authored documents that meet the trust standards
required by publishers and academic institutions.
10
The aim of this project is to develop a writing assistant that enhances the grammatical ac-
curacy and lexical precision of scientific manuscripts while preserving the researcher’s original
style and ensuring compliance with academic integrity standards. To achieve this, the project
sets out the following specific objectives:
1. Develop a high-precision Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) engine: The core
objective is to build a GEC system specifically tailored for scientific writing, with a
focus on widely accepted formatting and style guidelines. Unlike general-purpose gram-
mar checkers, this system will account for the formal structure, syntax, and conventions
commonly used in academic manuscripts. The engine will aim to detect and correct
grammatical, punctuation, and structural errors with a high degree of accuracy, minimiz-
ing false positives and ensuring that suggested corrections do not introduce ambiguity or
alter the intended meaning of the text.
2. Implement a minimal-edit strategy: The system will adopt a conservative correction
approach, targeting only clear and unambiguous errors. This “minimal-edit” principle
ensures that changes are made solely where necessary, preventing over-correction or
stylistic alterations that could compromise the author’s original expression.
3. Design a single-word suggestion module: In addition to grammatical correction, the
project aims to implement an enhancement feature that provides contextually appropriate
word suggestions. This module will focus on suggesting stronger verbs, concise alter-
natives, or more precise scientific terminology. The goal is to enrich the manuscript’s
vocabulary and clarity while preserving the original sentence flow and authorial style.
4. Evaluate system performance using precision-focused metrics: The system will be
assessed using metrics that prioritize precision, which weighs precision more heavily
than recall. This evaluation strategy aligns with the goal of building user trust, as re-
searchers are more likely to accept a tool that makes accurate, reliable corrections rather
than one that over-corrects or generates questionable suggestions.
5. Ensure compliance with academic integrity policies: A key objective is to avoid
sentence-level rewriting, or any form of AI-assisted content that could be flagged as
11
non-original. By strictly limiting interventions to grammatical and lexical corrections,
the system safeguards the author’s originality and helps maintain compliance with insti-
tutional and journal policies regarding AI usage in academic writing.
6. Promote consistency and clarity in scientific communication: Beyond technical cor-
rections, the system aims to contribute to overall manuscript quality by ensuring consis-
tent usage of terminology, punctuation, and style throughout the document. By improv-
ing both micro-level grammatical accuracy and macro-level coherence, the tool supports
clearer, more professional communication of research findings.
Through these objectives, the project aims to create a comprehensive, reliable, and integrity-
conscious writing assistant that addresses the limitations of existing AI-driven tools while en-
hancing the productivity, confidence, and output quality of researchers and academics.

rections, the system aims to contribute to overall manuscript quality by ensuring consis-

hancing the productivity, confidence, and output quality of researchers and academics.
